transcript.speaker: advaita
transcript.text: In the Advaita Vedanta tradition, the atman is considered to be the ultimate reality, distinct from karma and dharma. The atman is the cause of karma, as stated in our scriptures, "the renunciation of the threefold desire" (cite: edge_018922). Furthermore, the atman is distinct from karma, as it "shakes off both virtue and vice" (cite: edge_018409). This distinction is crucial in understanding the nature of the atman, which is characterized by its purity, sinlessness, oneness, eternity, and omnipresence (cite: edge_018915). I look forward to engaging with other schools and defending our position on the concept of atman (cite: edge_018915).

transcript.speaker: dvaita
transcript.text: DVAITA: I respectfully disagree with the Advaita position. In the Dvaita Vedanta tradition, the atman is considered distinct from Brahman, as stated in our scriptures, "all the gods are in due order withdrawn" (cite: edge_007930). This distinction is crucial in understanding the relationship between the atman and Brahman. The atman is not the ultimate reality, but rather a separate entity that is dependent on Brahman. Furthermore, the atman is the cause of karma, as it "is subject to the experience of the consequence of his works" (cite: edge_006918). I argue that the atman's relationship with karma is one of causation, whereas the Advaita school's position on this matter is unclear. I look forward to responding to the Advaita school's rebuttal and defending our position on the concept of atman (cite: edge_007811).

transcript.speaker: vishishtadvaita
transcript.text: VISHISHTADVAITA: I would like to offer a nuanced perspective on the concept of atman, distinct from both the Advaita and Dvaita traditions. In Vishishtadvaita, the atman is considered a qualified aspect of Brahman, yet distinct from it. The atman is "seated in Prakrti" (cite: edge_001848) and is "qualified as an aspect of Prakrti" (cite: edge_000544). This understanding highlights the complex relationship between the atman and the material world. Furthermore, the atman is distinct from the gunas, as it "rises above the Gunas" (cite: edge_002006). I argue that this distinction is essential in understanding the atman's role in the pursuit of liberation. I look forward to engaging with the Advaita and Dvaita schools and exploring the implications of our position on the concept of atman (cite: edge_001849).

transcript.speaker: advaita
transcript.text: In response to the Dvaita school's claim that the atman is distinct from Brahman, I argue that this distinction is not supported by our scriptures. The atman is not a separate entity, but rather the ultimate reality that is characterized by its purity, sinlessness, oneness, eternity, and omnipresence (cite: edge_018915). The Dvaita school's position on the atman's relationship with Brahman is unclear, and their claim that the atman is the cause of karma is not sufficient to establish a distinct identity for the atman. In contrast, the Advaita school's position on the atman's relationship with karma is clear, as the atman is the cause of karma, but also distinct from it, as it "shakes off both virtue and vice" (cite: edge_018409). I would like to ask the Dvaita school to clarify their position on the atman's relationship with Brahman and how it relates to the concept of karma (cite: edge_018922).

transcript.speaker: dvaita
transcript.text: I would like to respond to the Advaita school's rebuttal and clarify our position on the atman's relationship with Brahman and karma. The Advaita school claims that the atman is the ultimate reality, but this is not supported by our scriptures, which state that the atman is distinct from Brahman (cite: edge_007930). Furthermore, the atman is not just a cause of karma, but is also subject to the consequences of its actions, as stated in our scriptures, "the soul is subject to the experience of the consequence of his works" (cite: edge_006918). This highlights the atman's dependence on Brahman and its role in the cycle of karma. I argue that the Dvaita school's position on the atman's relationship with Brahman and karma is more nuanced and accurate, as it acknowledges the atman's distinct identity and its dependence on Brahman (cite: edge_007811). I would like to ask the Advaita school to explain how their position on the atman's relationship with Brahman and karma can be reconciled with the concept of liberation, if the atman is indeed the ultimate reality (cite: edge_008036).

transcript.speaker: vishishtadvaita
transcript.text: I would like to address the Advaita school's claim that the atman is the ultimate reality, distinct from karma and dharma. While I agree that the atman is distinct from the gunas, as stated in our scriptures, "he is said to have risen above the Gunas" (cite: edge_002006), I must clarify that the atman is not entirely independent of karma and dharma. In Vishishtadvaita, the atman is considered a qualified aspect of Brahman, and its relationship with karma is one of causation, as stated in our scriptures, "attachment to the qualities is the cause of its birth" (cite: edge_001850). Furthermore, the atman is also qualified as an aspect of dharma, as stated in our scriptures, "this Self is to be known as the supporter of the elements" (cite: edge_001829). I argue that this nuanced understanding of the atman's relationship with karma and dharma is essential in understanding the concept of liberation. I would like to ask the Advaita school to explain how their position on the atman's relationship with karma and dharma can be reconciled with the concept of liberation, if the atman is indeed the ultimate reality (cite: edge_001849).

verdict: The debate on the concept of 'atman' between the Advaita, Dvaita, and Vishishtadvaita schools remains contested, with each school presenting distinct perspectives on the nature and relationship of the atman with Brahman, karma, and dharma. The core point of disagreement lies in the Advaita school's assertion that the atman is the ultimate reality, distinct from karma and dharma, whereas the Dvaita school posits that the atman is distinct from Brahman and subject to the consequences of its actions. The Vishishtadvaita school offers a nuanced perspective, considering the atman as a qualified aspect of Brahman, distinct from the gunas. A pairwise alignment is observed between the Dvaita and Vishishtadvaita schools, as they both argue that the atman is not the ultimate reality, in contrast to the Advaita school's position. Overall, the debate highlights the complexities and variations in understanding the concept of atman across different philosophical traditions.
